title: Retry Strategies
concept: Automatically retrying transient failures (like network timeouts or rate limits) before involving the model.

exercises.prompt: Last lesson: why do we structure an error report with context, type, and a recommendation?

options.text: To help the model quickly understand what went wrong and how to fix it
To make the error message longer and harder to read
To hide the error from the user completely

exercises.explain: A clear, structured error report (what happened, what type, what to try) gives the model the clues it needs to find a smart solution instead of guessing.

cards.title: The blip that fixes itself
cards.body: Sometimes a request fails for a second just because the internet hiccuped. Wait a moment, try again, and it works! Not every error needs a big rescue plan.

cards.title: What is a retry strategy?
cards.body: A retry strategy is an automatic plan to try a failed action again a few times before asking the model for help. It targets transient failures—temporary problems that often clear up on their own.

cards.title: Like a dropped call
cards.body: If a phone call drops mid-sentence, you don't rewrite your whole plan—you just call back. Retrying a timed-out request is the same: a quick redial usually reconnects you.

cards.title: Backing off politely
cards.body: An agent hits a 'rate limit: too many requests' error. Instead of giving up, it waits 1 second, then 2, then 4 before retrying. This 'backoff' gives the server time to catch its breath.

cards.title: Myth: Retry everything
cards.body: Not every error should be retried! A 'wrong password' or 'file not found' won't fix itself—retrying just wastes time. Only retry transient errors like timeouts and rate limits.

cards.title: Transient vs. permanent
cards.body: Retry the temporary stuff. Escalate the real problems to the model.

left.text: Network timeout, rate limit, server busy—likely to clear up soon.

right.text: Bad password, missing file, invalid input—retrying won't help.

exercises.prompt: Which failure is a good candidate for an automatic retry?

options.text: A network request that timed out
A login with the wrong password
A request for a file that doesn't exist

exercises.explain: A timeout is transient—the connection may have just hiccuped, so trying again often succeeds. The others are permanent problems that retrying can't fix.

exercises.prompt: True or false: Waiting a little longer between each retry (backoff) helps avoid overwhelming a busy server.
exercises.explain: True! Increasing the wait time between retries (1s, 2s, 4s) gives an overloaded or rate-limited server time to recover instead of hammering it.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the blank about when to retry.
exercises.sentence: An agent should automatically retry transient failures a few times before it involves the model.
exercises.explain: Transient failures are temporary problems (like timeouts) that often clear up, so retrying makes sense. Permanent errors won't fix themselves.

exercises.prompt: Which retry log was written by an AI agent following a smart backoff strategy?

options.text: Rate limit hit. Retrying in 1s... failed. Retrying in 2s... failed. Retrying in 4s... success!
Rate limit hit. Retrying instantly 500 times in a row with no pause.
Rate limit hit. Giving up immediately and crashing the whole task.

exercises.explain: The AI-style agent used exponential backoff—waiting longer each time—then succeeded. Hammering instantly or giving up right away are both poor strategies.

exercises.prompt: An agent's tool call fails with 'HTTP 429: too many requests.' What's the best recovery plan?

options.text: Wait with increasing backoff and retry a few times; only escalate to the model if it still fails
Immediately stop the task and report a fatal error to the user
Retry instantly hundreds of times until it works
Ignore the error and pretend the call succeeded

exercises.explain: A 429 rate-limit error is transient, so the smart move is to retry with growing backoff a limited number of times. If it keeps failing, THEN hand a clear error report to the model. This saves the model's time for problems that actually need reasoning—handling routine blips automatically keeps agents fast and reliable.
